refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across representative worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is refined to be measurable, time-bound, and scenario-aware. It explicitly ties the functional requirement to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and acknowledges that energy efficiency is a system-level property dependent on operator behavior, vehicle design, and operational context. The refinement separates the capability (enabling efficiency) from the measurement (KPI achievement).
refined_fr.verification_idea: Establish a baseline energy consumption model; conduct controlled field trials across representative worksite scenarios (soil type, load profile, distance, operator skill level); measure energy_per_ton continuously via onboard telemetry; compare against E_target; document variance and root causes.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through optimized drivetrain, hydraulic, and engine efficiency.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall enable remote operators to execute tasks with energy efficiency comparable to or better than on-site operation.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and advisory feedback to operators to support energy-aware decision-making.
FR-EE-004: Safety Services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade energy_per_ton below acceptable thresholds when safety constraints are active.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system with real-time telemetry feedback, operator advisory, and vehicle-level efficiency optimization (engine load balancing, hydraulic pressure regulation, drivetrain mode selection).

DP-EE-B: Establish energy efficiency as a design constraint during vehicle development; optimize mechanical and hydraulic components for the target duty cycle; rely on operator training and worksite planning to achieve KPI.

DP-EE-C: Hybrid approach: vehicle-level efficiency optimization (DP-EE-B foundation) + real-time telemetry and operator advisory (DP-EE-A feedback loop) + energy-aware task scheduling and route optimization at the solution level.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Hybrid energy management combining vehicle-level mechanical/hydraulic optimization, real-time telemetry-driven operator advisory, and solution-level task/route optimization to achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

potential_sources: Vehicle efficiency (DP-EE-B component) is independent of teleoperation and safety systems; decoupled via standard interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking).
Telemetry feedback (DP-EE-A component) couples to operator behavior; managed via advisory (non-mandatory) and training.

Safety constraints (SafetyAlert) may couple to energy efficiency if safety-driven mode restrictions reduce efficiency; mitigated by design review and acceptance criteria that account for safety overhead.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C avoids tight coupling by layering: vehicle optimization is passive, telemetry is observational, and advisory is informational. Safety constraints are treated as boundary conditions, not as sources of uncontrolled coupling.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle drivetrain and hydraulic systems can be optimized to meet E_target without compromising safety or performance margins.
Assumption: Operators will respond to energy advisory feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
Risk: If vehicle mechanical efficiency is insufficient, no amount of telemetry feedback will achieve E_target; early vehicle design validation is critical.
Risk: Teleoperation latency or operator skill variance may degrade energy efficiency; requires operator training and latency specification.
Risk: Safety-driven mode restrictions (e.g., reduced speed, increased braking) may impose energy penalties; acceptance criteria must account for this.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-EE-C to explicitly specify: (1) vehicle efficiency targets (e.g., hydraulic efficiency ≥ 85%, engine load factor ≥ 0.7 under nominal duty), (2) telemetry update frequency and advisory algorithm (e.g., real-time energy rate display, predictive efficiency alerts), (3) task optimization scope (e.g., route planning, load consolidation), and (4) safety overhead budget (e.g., energy_per_ton target includes up to 10% safety margin).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level integration point responsible for coordinating vehicle efficiency, teleoperation energy awareness, and safety-compliant task execution. Owns the energy management strategy, telemetry aggregation, operator advisory logic, and KPI reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound energy consumption data (power, fuel rate, load, speed) from Vehicle; used to compute energy_per_ton and drive operator advisory.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound task directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation; inbound task completion status. Energy-aware task scheduling and route optimization logic resides here.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Inbound safety constraint notifications from Safety Services; used to adjust energy targets and advisory thresholds when safety modes are active.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for telemetry, tasking, and safety alert exchange; energy efficiency of network operations is out of scope but latency impacts operator efficiency.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural system-level owner of energy management strategy, KPI definition, and cross-module coordination. Energy advisory logic, task optimization, and telemetry aggregation are best centralized here. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and tasking dispatch cross module boundaries.

Vehicle owns mechanical/hydraulic efficiency optimization (DP-EE-B component) and real-time energy telemetry generation. However, energy_per_ton KPI computation, operator advisory, and task optimization belong at solution level. If energy management logic is distributed into Vehicle, it creates tight coupling and reduces flexibility for multi-vehicle fleets.

Teleoperation could own operator advisory display and energy-aware control recommendations. However, the core energy management strategy and KPI tracking should not reside here; doing so would fragment the energy model across on-site and remote operators and complicate fleet-level reporting.

Safety Services provides constraints (SafetyAlert) but should not own energy efficiency logic. Mixing safety and energy optimization creates conflicting objectives and complicates verification. Safety Services should be a constraint provider, not an energy manager.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency management is a system-level concern that requires coordination across Vehicle (mechanical efficiency), Teleoperation (operator behavior), and Safety Services (constraint boundaries). Centralizing the energy management strategy, KPI computation, and operator advisory in NGHE Solution ensures coherent policy, simplifies verification, and enables fleet-level optimization without duplicating logic across modules.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic coordinator, it may accumulate too many responsibilities and become difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: Define clear interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) and keep NGHE Solution focused on strategy and coordination, not low-level control.

Risk: Vehicle efficiency optimization (mechanical/hydraulic) must be validated independently; if Vehicle module does not meet efficiency targets, NGHE Solution cannot compensate. Mitigation: Establish vehicle efficiency acceptance criteria early and validate via component testing.

Risk: Teleoperation operator behavior is a major variable; if operators ignore energy advisory, KPI targets may not be met. Mitigation: Integrate advisory into operator training and task planning; consider mandatory energy-aware mode for critical tasks.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle: NGHE Solution owns energy management strategy, KPI tracking, and operator advisory; Vehicle owns mechanical/hydraulic efficiency optimization and telemetry generation. Define a formal energy telemetry interface (Telemetry) with specified fields (power, fuel rate, load, speed, efficiency ratio) and update frequency. Establish a task optimization interface (Tasking) that includes energy-aware scheduling hints (e.g., preferred route, load consolidation). Document the energy overhead budget for safety constraints in the acceptance criteria.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-EE-C (hybrid energy management) directly addresses FR-EE (energy efficiency KPI). The DP specifies a coherent strategy combining vehicle optimization, telemetry feedback, and operator advisory. The mapping is clear: vehicle efficiency reduces baseline consumption, telemetry enables visibility, and advisory supports operator decision-making. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Energy telemetry must flow from Vehicle to NGHE Solution; if telemetry is incomplete or delayed, advisory quality degrades. Mitigation: Define telemetry interface with mandatory fields and latency bounds.

Task optimization logic in NGHE Solution must coordinate with Vehicle and Teleoperation; if coordination is loose, energy-aware scheduling may not be executed. Mitigation: Formalize Tasking interface with energy-aware hints and confirmation feedback.

Safety constraints (SafetyAlert) may override energy optimization; if safety and energy objectives conflict, the design must prioritize safety. Mitigation: Document safety overhead budget in acceptance criteria and validate that energy_per_ton target is achievable under safety constraints.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner for energy management strategy and KPI tracking. Vehicle and Teleoperation are suppliers of data and executors of directives. Safety Services is a constraint provider. The module boundaries are clear and the interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy_per_ton KPI is measurable via telemetry (energy_used from Vehicle, ton_moved from task completion). Verification requires controlled field trials across representative scenarios. Acceptance criteria are quantitative (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and time-bound (over agreed evaluation period). Verification is feasible if telemetry is reliable and scenarios are well-defined.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-EE is allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level owner. Sub-FRs are allocated to Vehicle (mechanical efficiency), Teleoperation (remote operator efficiency), and Safety Services (constraint compliance). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and measurable acceptance criteria. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine the energy telemetry interface (Telemetry) to specify mandatory fields, update frequency, and accuracy requirements. Example: power (±5%), fuel rate (±3%), load (±2%), speed (±1%), update frequency ≥ 1 Hz.

Define the task optimization interface (Tasking) to include energy-aware scheduling hints and confirmation feedback. Example: preferred route, load consolidation, energy budget, actual energy consumed.
Establish a safety overhead budget in the acceptance criteria. Example: energy_per_ton target includes up to 10% overhead for safety-driven mode restrictions.

Document the evaluation period and representative scenarios for KPI verification. Example: 30-day field trial across 5 representative worksites (soil type, load profile, distance, operator skill level).
Specify operator training requirements for energy-aware operation and advisory interpretation.

decision.justification: FR-EE (energy efficiency KPI) is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing vehicle efficiency, teleoperation efficiency, energy visibility, and safety constraint compliance. DP-EE-C is sound and well-coupled, but its realization depends on successful decomposition and allocation of sub-FRs to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The next level of decomposition is necessary to detail the vehicle efficiency targets, telemetry interface, operator advisory algorithm, and task optimization logic. NGHE Solution is the correct IM owner, but its responsibilities must be clarified through sub-FR decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The vehicle shall minimize energy consumption per unit work output through optimized drivetrain, hydraulic, and engine efficiency.
FR-EE-002: The teleoperation system shall enable remote operators to execute tasks with energy efficiency comparable to or better than on-site operation.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and advisory feedback to operators to support energy-aware decision-making.
FR-EE-004: Safety Services shall not impose energy penalties that degrade energy_per_ton below acceptable thresholds when safety constraints are active.

assumptions: Vehicle drivetrain and hydraulic systems can be optimized to meet E_target without compromising safety or performance margins.
Operators will respond to energy advisory feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
Telemetry data is reliable, timely, and sufficient to compute energy_per_ton accurately.
Safety constraints are well-defined and their energy overhead is quantifiable and acceptable.
Evaluation period and representative scenarios are agreed with the customer and are representative of actual worksite conditions.
open_risks: Vehicle mechanical efficiency may be insufficient to meet E_target; early vehicle design validation is critical.
Teleoperation latency or operator skill variance may degrade energy efficiency; requires operator training and latency specification.
Safety-driven mode restrictions may impose energy penalties that exceed the acceptable overhead budget; requires design review and acceptance criteria adjustment.
Operator adoption of energy advisory may be low if advisory is not integrated into task planning and training; requires change management and incentive alignment.
Multi-vehicle fleet heterogeneity may complicate energy optimization; requires fleet-level energy model and per-vehicle calibration.
evidence_needed: Vehicle efficiency baseline: measured energy consumption under nominal duty cycle (e.g., standard load, distance, terrain).
Telemetry interface specification: mandatory fields, update frequency, accuracy, latency.
Operator advisory algorithm: energy rate display, predictive efficiency alerts, decision support logic.
Task optimization algorithm: route planning, load consolidation, energy-aware scheduling.
Safety overhead budget: quantified energy penalty for safety-driven mode restrictions.
Evaluation protocol: field trial design, scenario definition, measurement methodology, acceptance criteria.
Operator training plan: energy-aware operation, advisory interpretation, task planning.
